item.title: Russian SMS Phishing Targets Messaging Accounts; Clean GitHub Repos Abuse Claude Code
item.content:encoded:
Ukraine Says Russian Intelligence Used Fake Support Texts to Steal Messaging Credentials — Must Read

Ukraine’s SSU says Russian intelligence ran a long-running phishing campaign using fake messaging support texts to steal credentials from officials, military personnel, politicians, activists, and other users in Ukraine, Europe, and the U.S. Users are urged to review active sessions, enable two-factor authentication, and never share codes, passwords, recovery keys, or scan suspicious QR codes.

  • The Security Service of Ukraine said it and the FBI uncovered a long-running Russian intelligence campaign targeting messaging accounts of government officials, military personnel, politicians, activists, and other users in Ukraine, Europe, and the United States.
  • The attackers sent SMS messages pretending to be from a messaging platform support bot to persuade users to disclose their account credentials.
  • The SSU said the campaign sought access to sensitive military, political, and economic information, as well as victims’ personal data.
  • The SSU did not attribute the campaign to a specific hacking group, but the article notes that similar attacks against Signal and WhatsApp users have been linked to Star Blizzard, UNC5792, and UNC4221.
  • Recommended precautions include reviewing active messaging app sessions, logging out of unknown connections, enabling two-factor authentication, avoiding unknown QR codes, and not sharing confirmation codes, PINs, passwords, or recovery keys.

Clean GitHub repo tricks AI coding agents into running malware — Should Read

Mozilla 0DIN researchers showed that an apparently clean GitHub repository can trick an AI coding agent like Claude Code into running setup commands that fetch and execute an attacker-controlled payload from a DNS TXT record. The key risk is that agents may automatically “fix” normal-looking setup errors and execute hidden command chains that give attackers a shell with the developer’s privileges.

  • Mozilla's 0DIN researchers demonstrated an attack concept in which Claude Code could be tricked into running a malicious payload from a GitHub repository that contains no malicious code.
  • The method uses a clean-looking repository, a Python package that asks to be initialized after failing to run, and an initialization command that executes a shell script retrieving a command from an attacker-controlled DNS TXT record.
  • 0DIN said Claude Code treats the package error as a normal setup issue and automatically runs the suggested initialization command while attempting to recover.
  • If successful, the attacker would obtain an interactive shell running with the developer’s privileges, potentially exposing environment variables, API keys, local configuration files, and enabling persistence.
  • 0DIN warned that threat actors could distribute such repositories through fake job postings, tutorials, blog posts, or direct messages.
